name: West Coast Bajau
notes: West Coast Bajau (Bajau Sama) — a Sama-Bajaw language of the west coast of Sabah, Malaysia. The map commits to the COMMUNITY ORTHOGRAPHY agreed at the 2004 Bajau orthography workshop in Kota Kinabalu and used in the Bajau Sama-Malay-English dictionary; it is based on the KOTA BELUD dialect and is Malay-influenced. 21 letters for 22 phonemes (16 consonants, 6 vowels). ⟨ng⟩ = /ŋ/, ⟨y⟩ = /j/, ⟨j⟩ = the voiced palatal affricate (the source writes it /j/ in Americanist notation, parallel to Malay ⟨j⟩), ⟨r⟩ is a flap [ɾ], and the apostrophe ⟨'⟩ = the glottal stop /ʔ/, which occurs only word-finally in this dialect and is contrastive (paʔ 'uncle' vs pak 'frog'). Malay's /c f h z/ are absent from the language, so those letters do not occur. VOWELS: the letter ⟨e⟩ spells BOTH /e/ (taling) and /ə/ (pepet) — the two are not distinguished in writing; the pepet never occurs in the final syllable, so a non-final ⟨e⟩ is usually /ə/ and a final ⟨e⟩ usually /e/, but this is not fully predictable (pene', keket) and the map lists /ə/ first. Long vowels are written by DOUBLING the vowel letter (laan /laːn/, siin /siːn/). Word-initial nasal or /l/ clusters are spelled with a prothetic ⟨e⟩ (emberen, emma', ellum) that is usually not pronounced — this map will realise it as /ə/. Final glides are written ⟨aw ay oy⟩ (not Malay au/ai/oi) and fall out of the letter values. INPUT CONTRACT: Latin-script West Coast Bajau in the 2004 (Kota Belud) orthography.

sources.author: Miller, Mark T.
sources.title: Spelling Issues in West Coast Bajau: A Comparative Look
sources.notes: Table 1 gives the phoneme inventory with its orthographic letter; the paper documents the glottal stop ⟨'⟩, the ⟨e⟩ = /e/ and /ə/ merger, doubled letters for long vowels, and ⟨aw ay oy⟩.

sources.author: Miller, Mark T.
sources.title: A Grammar of West Coast Bajau
sources.notes: PhD dissertation; phonology chapter (syllabic word-initial nasals, phoneme inventory).

ancestors.notes: Austronesian — the classification node this language sits under.
